4: E-mail addresses: valentina.fantin@enea.it (V. Fantin), antonio.giuliano@enea.it (A. Giuliano), michele.manfredi@unipr.it (M. Manfredi), giuseppe.ottaviano@enea. it (G. Ottaviano), milena.stefanova@enea.it (M. Stefanova), paolo.masoni@enea.it (P. Masoni).

The interest in biogas production from anaerobic digestion (AD) used for electricity and heating production or as a substitute for fossil fuel applications has increased rapidly over the last years, since it can help to reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and improve national energy security [1]. In Italy, for example, the favourable incentives for the production of electricity from AD plants (up to 280 €/MWh for plants built until 1st January 2013), has led to a proliferation of biogas plants (1239 operating plants in June 2014, with a total installed capacity of 955 MW and an elec- tricity production of 5.7 TWh in 2013 [2]). Most of these biogas plants are located in Northern Italy and fed with livestock effluents and energy crops [3].
Adoption of biogas technology does not necessarily lead to sustainable practices. In fact, climate change mitigation potential of biogas can be counteracted by an increase in the GHG emissions

during energy crops cultivation [4], biogas plant operation, its uti- lization and transport, storage and use of digestate [5]. More in particular, the addition of energy crops enhances the performance of the digesters by balancing the C/N of the substrate, but on the other hand they are an unfavourable input material from an envi- ronmental sustainability point of view, because of the high resource consumption required for their cultivation [6]. Moreover, while the use of residual digestate as fertilizer can reduce the dependence on chemical fertilisers, anaerobic digestion destroys the proteins contained in the biomass, determining "ammonification" which results in higher ammonia levels in the digestate compared to an- imal slurry, with consequent remarkable ammonia emissions when digestate is stored and spread on agricultural fields [5,7]. Conse- quently, the environmental sustainability of biogas production and use should be properly evaluated by means of scientific and robust methods, such as Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), which enables assessing complete bioenergy production chains throughout their life cycle [8,9]. Several LCA studies on biogas production have been published over the last years with different specific goals and analysing different issues. Fuchsz and Kohlheb [10], Lijo et al. [11,12] and Righi et al. [13] assess the environmental consequences of some German and Italian biogas plants respectively. Buratti et al. [14] focus their analysis only on GHG emissions, whereas several authors assess the environmental performance of biogas systems using different kind of input materials (maize or grass silage, manure, crop residues or a combination of these materials) [5,15-22]. Some studies are focused on the comparison of the environmental performance among different crops dedicated to biomass production [23,24]. Jury et al. [25] evaluates the fermen- tation of energy crops, the production and purification of biogas for the production of biomethane and its injection into the natural gas grid. All these studies report a mitigation of GHG emissions by biogas production, although the results show that biogas plants can increase the risk of acidification and eutrophication, mainly due to digestate storage in open tanks and its agronomic use [5,18,21]. However, the results of the above studies are difficult to compare because of the different functional units and system boundaries, different assumptions and methodological choices, such as the models for calculating emissions from the digestate management, various replaced reference systems and different life cycle impact assessment methods. Particular focus must be given to the choice of model for estimating nitrogen emissions from the digestate storage and use, because it can significantly affect LCA results. In fact, since their direct measure is complex and not frequently feasible within the limited scope of an LCA study, these emissions are usually estimated by means of literature models, though no scientific consensus on what model should be adopted currently exists. The aim of the paper is to present a state-of-art LCA study of electricity produced in an AD plant located in Northern Italy, with the goal to identify the environmental hotspots and produce recommendations on how to mitigate some of them effectively. As required by the ISO standard, but not always applied in published LCA studies, the robustness and reliability of the results were checked with a sensitivity analysis and an uncertainty analysis. The sensitivity analysis was focused on the effects on final results of four different models available in literature for the calculation of nitrogen emissions from the storage and spreading of the diges- tate. The four methods (two of which based on experimental re- sults and two proposed in technical guidelines) were selected with the help of a literature survey, reviewing how published LCA studies on biogas production dealt with the calculation of these emissions. Finally, the uncertainty of the LCA results was assessed by means of a Monte Carlo analysis, drawing the probability density function and the 95% confidence interval for each impact category.

6: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>
2.1. Operation and mass balance of the anaerobic digestion plant

The biological phase of the AD plant assessed in the study is based on a continuous two stage fed process, coupled with a fully covered gas tight storage tank for treated materials (i.e. digestate). The AD plant adopts a co-digestion approach treating dedicated energy crops (maize silage, triticale and sorghum) mixed with cow slurry and agro-residues (pressed sugar beet pulps and winery waste). The daily composition of the input energy crops and agro- residues depends on their seasonal availability. Energy crops and agro-residues are characterized by a total solids (TS) content of 24.7-35% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction more than 90% suggesting a high biodegradability of the solid materials (Table On the contrary, as expected, cow slurry shows a lower solid content (80 g TS kg-1, 76% volatile). Agro-residues show a similar concentration of total nitrogen (Ntot) (about 1.2 whereas for energy crops and cow slurry this is in the range 2.8-4.4 Nkg-1. Most of the ammonia nitrogen (TAN) entering the biological phase comes from cow slurry which shows a concen- tration of 1.1 g NH4 (Table

The plant is operated in wet conditions at mesophilic temper- ature (40-42 °C) with an Organic Loading Rate (OLR) of about 1.8 kg Total Volatile Solids (TVS) m day-1 and Hydraulic Reten- tion Time (HRT) of 100 days. The biogas produced (3,898,000 m3 year-1 is burned in two combined heat and power units (CHP), with a total installed power of 998 kW (Table 1).

For the operating period (2012), the mass balance of the AD plant was checked considering the mass of feeding substrate, the mass of the produced biogas and the mass of digestate (Table 1). According to Schievano et al. [26], this method requires a consistent number of samples of both input and output materials, throughout a long period of observation, to avoid sampling errors, sample variability and time-scale variation. In this study the difference between the input and output materials was found to be accept- able, i.e. lower than 5% for all the parameters considered (total solids and nitrogen).
The mass balance did not include the fraction of water and ammonia contained in the produced biogas since they are negli- gible if compared to the main mass flows.

The amount of TS treated by the AD plant is about 6181 tonne year (5791 tonne year of TVS) with an overall biogas produc- tion of 5123 tonne year The digestate (17,836 tonne year-1 shows an average TS content of 6.9% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction of 72% (on TS). Therefore, during the overall operating period, about 80% of solids contained in the input materials are converted into biogas, corresponding to TVS reduction of 84%. The high rate of biodegradability of the feeding materials is confirmed also observing the fraction of TAN with respect to the Ntot in the influent and effluent streams (Fig. 1).

In fact, the total nitrogen enters the anaerobic reactors mainly in its organic form (95% of the total nitrogen) and then undergoes to hydrolysis and ammonification processes during digestion phase. The ammonification process of the particulate organic nitrogen of the substrate is one of the main drawbacks of the AD process, since it enhances the potential for NH3 emission during the digestate storage if compared to the untreated liquid slurry. On the other hand, the reduced organic matter increases the permeability into the soil as well as the nitrogen uptake of the crops reducing, therefore, the potential for NH3 losses after field application [27,28]. The fraction of TAN in the digestate is 56% in mass of the total ni- trogen in the effluent.
Before being recycled on agricultural fields as organic fertilizer, the digestate is sent to a liquid/solid separator and the resulted

After separation phase, 90% of the raw digestate is separated as liquid fraction with a TS content of about 55 g kg whereas the remaining part (i.e. the solid fraction), shows a TS concentration of about 250 g kg- (Table 2).

The largest part of ammonia contained in the raw digestate is included in the liquid fraction, which shows TAN concentration of 2.5 g kg-1 (59% of Ntot). As regards the solid fraction, 69% of the Ntot is included in the organic form while TAN concentration is 1.8 g kg The Ntot concentrations of liquid and solid digestate are 4.3 and 5.8 g kg-1 ,respectively.
2.2. Goal and scope of the LCA study

2.2.1. Goal of the LCA study and target audience The goals of the LCA study were to assess the environmental performance of electricity production from the AD plant throughout its life cycle as well as to evaluate the hotspots in the production system and produce some recommendations for their mitigation. The target audience of the study are the AD plant managers.

After separation phase, 90% of the raw digestate is separated as liquid fraction with a TS content of about 55 g kg whereas the remaining part (i.e. the solid fraction), shows a TS concentration of about 250 g kg- (Table 2).

2.2. Goal and scope of the LCA study

2.2.1. Goal of the LCA study and target audience The goals of the LCA study were to assess the environmental performance of electricity production from the AD plant throughout its life cycle as well as to evaluate the hotspots in the production system and produce some recommendations for their mitigation. The target audience of the study are the AD plant managers.
8: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>
2.1. Operation and mass balance of the anaerobic digestion plant

The biological phase of the AD plant assessed in the study is based on a continuous two stage fed process, coupled with a fully covered gas tight storage tank for treated materials (i.e. digestate). The AD plant adopts a co-digestion approach treating dedicated energy crops (maize silage, triticale and sorghum) mixed with cow slurry and agro-residues (pressed sugar beet pulps and winery waste). The daily composition of the input energy crops and agro- residues depends on their seasonal availability. Energy crops and agro-residues are characterized by a total solids (TS) content of 24.7-35% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction more than 90% suggesting a high biodegradability of the solid materials (Table On the contrary, as expected, cow slurry shows a lower solid content (80 g TS kg-1, 76% volatile). Agro-residues show a similar concentration of total nitrogen (Ntot) (about 1.2 whereas for energy crops and cow slurry this is in the range 2.8-4.4 Nkg-1. Most of the ammonia nitrogen (TAN) entering the biological phase comes from cow slurry which shows a concen- tration of 1.1 g NH4 (Table

The plant is operated in wet conditions at mesophilic temper- ature (40-42 °C) with an Organic Loading Rate (OLR) of about 1.8 kg Total Volatile Solids (TVS) m day-1 and Hydraulic Reten- tion Time (HRT) of 100 days. The biogas produced (3,898,000 m3 year-1 is burned in two combined heat and power units (CHP), with a total installed power of 998 kW (Table 1).

For the operating period (2012), the mass balance of the AD plant was checked considering the mass of feeding substrate, the mass of the produced biogas and the mass of digestate (Table 1). According to Schievano et al. [26], this method requires a consistent number of samples of both input and output materials, throughout a long period of observation, to avoid sampling errors, sample variability and time-scale variation. In this study the difference between the input and output materials was found to be accept- able, i.e. lower than 5% for all the parameters considered (total solids and nitrogen).
The mass balance did not include the fraction of water and ammonia contained in the produced biogas since they are negli- gible if compared to the main mass flows.

The amount of TS treated by the AD plant is about 6181 tonne year (5791 tonne year of TVS) with an overall biogas produc- tion of 5123 tonne year The digestate (17,836 tonne year-1 shows an average TS content of 6.9% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction of 72% (on TS). Therefore, during the overall operating period, about 80% of solids contained in the input materials are converted into biogas, corresponding to TVS reduction of 84%. The high rate of biodegradability of the feeding materials is confirmed also observing the fraction of TAN with respect to the Ntot in the influent and effluent streams (Fig. 1).

In fact, the total nitrogen enters the anaerobic reactors mainly in its organic form (95% of the total nitrogen) and then undergoes to hydrolysis and ammonification processes during digestion phase. The ammonification process of the particulate organic nitrogen of the substrate is one of the main drawbacks of the AD process, since it enhances the potential for NH3 emission during the digestate storage if compared to the untreated liquid slurry. On the other hand, the reduced organic matter increases the permeability into the soil as well as the nitrogen uptake of the crops reducing, therefore, the potential for NH3 losses after field application [27,28]. The fraction of TAN in the digestate is 56% in mass of the total ni- trogen in the effluent.
Before being recycled on agricultural fields as organic fertilizer, the digestate is sent to a liquid/solid separator and the resulted

2.2. Goal and scope of the LCA study

2.2.1. Goal of the LCA study and target audience The goals of the LCA study were to assess the environmental performance of electricity production from the AD plant throughout its life cycle as well as to evaluate the hotspots in the production system and produce some recommendations for their mitigation. The target audience of the study are the AD plant managers.
13: <header>2. Materials and methods </header>
2.1. Operation and mass balance of the anaerobic digestion plant

The biological phase of the AD plant assessed in the study is based on a continuous two stage fed process, coupled with a fully covered gas tight storage tank for treated materials (i.e. digestate). The AD plant adopts a co-digestion approach treating dedicated energy crops (maize silage, triticale and sorghum) mixed with cow slurry and agro-residues (pressed sugar beet pulps and winery waste). The daily composition of the input energy crops and agro- residues depends on their seasonal availability. Energy crops and agro-residues are characterized by a total solids (TS) content of 24.7-35% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction more than 90% suggesting a high biodegradability of the solid materials (Table On the contrary, as expected, cow slurry shows a lower solid content (80 g TS kg-1, 76% volatile). Agro-residues show a similar concentration of total nitrogen (Ntot) (about 1.2 whereas for energy crops and cow slurry this is in the range 2.8-4.4 Nkg-1. Most of the ammonia nitrogen (TAN) entering the biological phase comes from cow slurry which shows a concen- tration of 1.1 g NH4 (Table

The plant is operated in wet conditions at mesophilic temper- ature (40-42 °C) with an Organic Loading Rate (OLR) of about 1.8 kg Total Volatile Solids (TVS) m day-1 and Hydraulic Reten- tion Time (HRT) of 100 days. The biogas produced (3,898,000 m3 year-1 is burned in two combined heat and power units (CHP), with a total installed power of 998 kW (Table 1).

For the operating period (2012), the mass balance of the AD plant was checked considering the mass of feeding substrate, the mass of the produced biogas and the mass of digestate (Table 1). According to Schievano et al. [26], this method requires a consistent number of samples of both input and output materials, throughout a long period of observation, to avoid sampling errors, sample variability and time-scale variation. In this study the difference between the input and output materials was found to be accept- able, i.e. lower than 5% for all the parameters considered (total solids and nitrogen).
The mass balance did not include the fraction of water and ammonia contained in the produced biogas since they are negli- gible if compared to the main mass flows.

The amount of TS treated by the AD plant is about 6181 tonne year (5791 tonne year of TVS) with an overall biogas produc- tion of 5123 tonne year The digestate (17,836 tonne year-1 shows an average TS content of 6.9% (on wet weight) with a volatile fraction of 72% (on TS). Therefore, during the overall operating period, about 80% of solids contained in the input materials are converted into biogas, corresponding to TVS reduction of 84%. The high rate of biodegradability of the feeding materials is confirmed also observing the fraction of TAN with respect to the Ntot in the influent and effluent streams (Fig. 1).

In fact, the total nitrogen enters the anaerobic reactors mainly in its organic form (95% of the total nitrogen) and then undergoes to hydrolysis and ammonification processes during digestion phase. The ammonification process of the particulate organic nitrogen of the substrate is one of the main drawbacks of the AD process, since it enhances the potential for NH3 emission during the digestate storage if compared to the untreated liquid slurry. On the other hand, the reduced organic matter increases the permeability into the soil as well as the nitrogen uptake of the crops reducing, therefore, the potential for NH3 losses after field application [27,28]. The fraction of TAN in the digestate is 56% in mass of the total ni- trogen in the effluent.
Before being recycled on agricultural fields as organic fertilizer, the digestate is sent to a liquid/solid separator and the resulted

2.2. Goal and scope of the LCA study

2.2.1. Goal of the LCA study and target audience The goals of the LCA study were to assess the environmental performance of electricity production from the AD plant throughout its life cycle as well as to evaluate the hotspots in the production system and produce some recommendations for their mitigation. The target audience of the study are the AD plant managers.
14: The main function of the studied system is electricity supply to the medium voltage national grid. The functional unit was

Fig. 1. Annual average of Nitrogen distribution with respect to the total nitrogen concentration in the input (raw materials), output (raw digestate), liquid fraction (digestate after separation) and solid fraction (digestate after separation).

therefore 1 MJ of net electricity produced. In some AD plants, the main function is the treatment of waste: this is not the case of the examined plant, as demonstrated by the large use of energy crops.
Since the decision context is micro level decision support, an attributional modelling was adopted [29].

System boundaries included the production of dedicated energy crops, transport and handling of all input materials, capital goods productions, the use of biogas in a CHP plant for electricity pro- duction as well as management of the digestate (Fig. 2).

Cut-off criteria were established as 1% threshold for mass and environmental impacts. Auxiliary products used for the opera- tion of the AD plant (i.e. carbon filters for H2S purification of biogas and enzymes for the silage process) were excluded due to their low amount and because their contribution to total envi- ronmental impacts of 1 MJ of electricity was lower than 1%. More in detail, the environmental impacts of carbon filters were esti- mated using Munöz [30] and considering an average consump- tion of 1200 kg of activated carbon per year (primary data from the AD plant). As regards enzymes, they were used only for triticale (0.2 kg per 100 tonne of triticale) and their environ- mental contribution was found to be negligible according to Nielsen et al. [31]]. Finally, distribution and use of electricity were not taken into account.

17: Electricity and heat are the outputs of the system. The former is exported to the national grid, whereas a certain amount of the latter is used internally for heating purposes. Since surplus heat is not exploited in any other way and is emitted in the air as waste heat, all impacts were attributed to the electricity production and no allocation was applied in the study.

In the upstream phase the system uses co-products and wastes supplied from other product systems (i.e. the livestock and other agro-food systems, e.g. beet sugar or wine production systems). The following allocation methods were used:

<list>0% impacts were allocated to outputs of other systems, consid- ered as waste streams, i.e. the slurry from the livestock system and the winery waste coming from wine production. 
Impacts of the pressed sugar beet pulps were allocated from the sugar production systems by applying economic allocation based on their average prices. The amount of co-products (sugar, beet pulps, molasses) obtained from 1 ton of sugar beets were estimated using Jungbuth et al. [32]. As regards market prices, primary data provided by plant managers were used. The above data and the allocation factors are included in Table 3.</list>
18: The midpoint impact assessment methods recommended by ILCD Handbook [32] were used in the study, focussing on the

following environmental impact categories: Global Warming (GW), Ozone Depletion (OD), Photochemical Ozone Formation (POF), Acidification (AC), Freshwater Eutrophication (F-EU), Marine Eutrophication (M-EU), Mineral, Fossil and Renewable resource Depletion (MFRD).

The environmental impacts of the biogas plant on biodiversity and land use were excluded, since consolidated impact methods for the above categories are still missing [32]. Moreover, toxicity emissions from the use of pesticides were not taken into account due to the following reasons: 1) the low robustness of USEtox model, as specified by the ILCD Handbook [33]; 2) the lack of characterisation factors for many active ingredients of pesticides; 3) no scientific consensus on what model should be used to calculate on-field emissions from pesticides at the inventory level.
19: The data for the foreground processes are technology-specific, whereas the background processes are characterised by Italian or European production mixes. Ecoinvent v.2.0 database [34] and literature were used for the background data. The Italian electricity production mix of ELCD 3.0 database [35] was used.
20: Specific primary data, referred to 2012, were collected from the AD plant and included primary data on input materials production and handling, anaerobic digestion process, electricity and heat

Table 1 shows the types of substrates used in the AD plant process and the respective yearly amount. All resource and energy consumption as well as emissions occurring during cultivation phase of energy crops were taken into account (Table 4). Data on sugar beets production were taken from Ecoinvent 2.0 database [34]. Emissions generated by diesel use were estimated in agree- ment with Nemecek and Kägi [36]. Nitrogen emissions to air and water due to the use of chemical fertilisers were estimated on the basis of IPCC 2006 emission factors [37] (Table 4). CO2 airborne emissions due to urea application and phosphorus waterborne emissions were calculated according to Nemecek and Kägi [36].

Input materials are transported from farms to the plant by tractor with trailer. For each input material, the actual distances (5-10 km on average for both energy crops and agro-wastes and 54 km for beet pulps) and the quantity of input materials trans- ported were taken into account. The diesel consumption for feedstock handling, pressing and loading is equal to 10,500 L year The environmental burdens associated to the production and consumption of both diesel and lubricant oils were taken into account.

The electricity consumption required for the plant operations is partially satisfied by 9.3% of the total electricity production. Remaining electricity requirements are satisfied by purchasing electricity from the grid (70,500 kWh/year). The consumption of water for the anaerobic digestion process is 130 m³. Methane leakages were estimated 1% of the biogas produced [38]. Pro- duction of infrastructures and equipment was taken into account using Ecoinvent v.2.0 data, the actual total volume of anaerobic digestion tanks (13800 m³) and the actual plant service life (15 years). Biogenic carbon sequestration during crops growth and the emissions released during the combustion of biogas were not taken into account because carbon removal was considered equal to the carbon contained in the biogas [17,39]. NO and CO airborne emissions due to the combustion of biogas in the cogeneration engine were calculated from primary data supplied by the plant managers and by the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio, whereas Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) and SO2 emissions were estimated according to Chevalier and Meunier [17] (Table 5). Primary data on the consumption of lubricant oil for the engines were collected, on the basis of the yearly working hours and is equal to 5800 L year Supplementary data A details the inventory data per 1 MJ of electricity and contains the Ecoinvent and ELCD (the latter only for purchased electricity) datasets used in the modelling phase.

NO and CO airborne emissions due to the combustion of biogas in the cogeneration engine were calculated from primary data supplied by the plant managers and by the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio, whereas Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC) and SO2 emissions were estimated according to Chevalier and Meunier [17] (Table 5). Table 1 shows the types of substrates used in the AD plant process and the respective yearly amount. All resource and energy consumption as well as emissions occurring during cultivation phase of energy crops were taken into account (Table 4). 26: <header>4. Methane and nitrogen emissions due to digestate storage and agronomic use </header>

The residual digestate, due to its high nutrient content, can be used as a fertiliser on agricultural fields, although this can lead to nitrogen airborne and waterborne emissions during storage and field application [40]. Moreover, the anaerobic digestion process continues during digestate storage, and methane emissions can occur [14]. In LCA studies, the measurement of these emissions is difficult and not usually viable within the available budget and time constraints. Therefore, these emissions shall be estimated using models, though no scientific consensus on what models should be adopted currently exists. Therefore, a literature survey was carried out, analysing how published LCA studies on biogas production dealt with the calculation of these emissions.

Some authors do not include the digestate in the system boundaries [17] or do not provide information on how they calculated nitrogen and methane emissions from the management of digestate [16,25]. The experimental data by Amon et al. [27,28], rescaled according to the Volatile Solid (VS) and nitrogen content of the feedstock were used by Boulamanti et al. [5]. Dressler et al. [41] adopt IPCC (2006) [37], whereas Battini et al. [38] utilise a combi- nation of Amon et al. [27,28], IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) [42] models, assimilating the digestate to manure. A combination of IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) were used by Fuchsz and Kohl- heb [10] whereas some papers adopt a combination of IPCC (2006) and other models or national inventories on ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions [15,19-21]. Finally, the models of Nemecek and Kägi [36] were used by De Meester et al. [18] and a combination of Brentrup et al. [43] and Rossier [44] were used by Lijó et al. [11,12].

From the survey it is clear that the most used models in LCA studies are those recommended by IPCC 2006 guidelines Tier 1 or 2 [37]. However, both these models and those in EMEP/EEA [42] have been developed to calculate emissions from the storage and use of organic fertilisers such as animal manure or compost. Moreover, both IPCC and EMEP/EEA models are based on statistical analyses with simplistic assumptions and they do not take explicitly into account all the relevant factors causing the emissions (pH, tem- perature, etc.). Anyway, some papers use data obtained by experi- mental measures. More in particular, Amon [27,28,45] measured N- emissions from not separated digestate (stored in a tank covered by wooden lid or straw) obtained from cattle slurry in an Austrian plant. Finally, Dinuccio et al. [46] measured N emissions from storage (in an open tank) and spreading of separated digestate obtained by an Italian AD plant using a mixture of energy crops, agricultural residues and cow slurry as input materials.

Tier 2 IPCC 2006 [37] approach for manure management, which is an international recognized method widely used by several au- thors as seen above, was adopted in the present study for the calculation of methane emissions due to the storage of digestate. Moreover, Tier 2 is a detailed approach based on specific primary data of the digestate. In fact, the calculation was performed considering primary data about the residual methane potential production of the raw (i.e. not separated) digested material (26.6
Nm³ per ton of VS) and by weighting it on the basis of the amount of volatile matter stored per year for each separated fractions (Table 2).

N20 and NH3 airborne emissions as well as NO3 waterborne emissions from digestate management were calculated with two methods proposed by technical guidelines and two experimental methods, and their effect on the final results was assessed with a sensitivity analysis. In particular the methods were:

<list>IPCC (2006) Tier 1 approach [37], using the default emission factors for cattle manure management, which was adopted for the base case. Direct N20 airborne emissions occurring during the storage of digestate are produced by nitrification and deni- trification of nitrogen contained in digestate. Calculation was based on the total amount of nitrogen contained in liquid and solid digestate and the emission factor regarding manure management system (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N for liquid manure stored in open tanks and 0.005 kg N20-N per kg N for solid manure stored in unconfined piles). Indirect N20 emissions from the volatilisation of digestate during storage were calcu- lated assuming that 40% of the digestate nitrogen is volatilised as NH3 and NOx and multiplying this value by the emission factor for N20 emissions from atmospheric deposition of ni- trogen on soils and water surfaces (0.01 kg N20-N per kg NH3-N volatilized). NH3-N emissions were estimated equal to 40% of the nitrogen content in the digestate, since IPCC approach does not provide sufficient information on how to separate NH3 and NO emissions. Direct N20 emissions resulting from the application to soils of nitrogen contained in digestate were calculated considering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied is released as N20. Nitrogen available for spreading was calculated by subtracting from the total nitrogen content of the digestate the nitrogen emitted as N20 and NH3 during storage. Indirect N20 emissions occur through two pathways (IPCC, 2006): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in digestate, followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO-N per kg N applied). In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and pro- duce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilisation and leaching/runoff respectively. 
EMEP/EEA (2013) Tier 2 approach [42], using the emission fac- tors for cattle manure and slurry. Since the mass balance approach has been applied, not only the NH3 and NOx emissions accounted under the EMEP/EEA method were estimated, but also all the N-containing species lost during storage. More pre- cisely, the estimated emissions include also N20 in air and leaching emissions of NO3 from the solid fraction (both in compliance with the IPCC Tier 1 methodology). For the phase of digestate spreading, the Tier 2 methodology is not based on complete mass balances and gives indications on how to compute only NH3 and NO emissions from soils. In order to complete the LCI data regarding N emissions, IPCC 2006 Tier 1 methodology was applied for estimation of N20 and NO3 emissions. Calculation of NH3 emissions during storage of digestate was based on the TAN content of digestate and the default Tier 2 emission EMEP/EEA factor regarding management of liquid and solid manure from cattle slurry (0.20 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.27 kg N-NH3/kg TAN respectively). Direct N20 emissions for storage were calculated considering the default Tier 1 emission factors of IPCC 2006 for slurry with natural crust</list>

<list>and solid manure, both related to TAN (0.008 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.016 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emis- sions were calculated as fractions of NH3 and NO3 emission (as in Tier 1 IPCC approach). As regards NO emissions, the default EMEP/EEA emission factors (0.0001 kg N-NO/kg TAN and 0.01 kg N-NO/kg TAN for storage of liquid and solid manure respectively) have been used. NO3 leaching from the storage of solid digestate were not included as emissions lost from the system, as it is assumed that leaching from the solid storage is recycled back in the digester plant. As regards spreading, NH3 emissions were estimated by using the default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 emission factors (0.79 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.55 kg N-NH3/kg TAN) regarding spreading of cattle manure and slurry respec- tively. Direct N20 emissions were based on the default Tier 1 emission factor of IPCC 2006 for cattle manure and slurry related to TAN (0.087 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.018 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emissions were calculated as frac- tions from NH3 and NO3 emission following the Tier 1 of the IPCC approach. For NO emissions, EMEP/EEA default Tier 1 emission factors were applied (0.026 kg N-NO/kg TAN for both slurry and manure). For NO3 leaching, the Tier 1 emission factor from IPCC 2006 was applied (0.3 kg N-N03/kg N). 
An adapted method (Method A) using the data reported by Dinuccio et al. [46], where the gaseous emissions from the solid and liquid fractions of the digestate during the storage and spreading phases were measured experimentally both in sum- mer and winter conditions. The amount of gaseous emission of ammonia into atmosphere depends on the gas-liquid phase equilibrium of ammonia nitrogen (NH4 NH3 + H+), where the ratio between unionized ammonia (NH3) and ammonium (NH4) is strictly linked to pH, temperature and ionic strength. Therefore, similarly to the approach reported by Boulamanti et al. [5], the ammonia losses during storage were estimated by scaling the literature data according to the unionized ammonia calculated with the formula given by Béline et al. [47], whereas N20 emissions were estimated by scaling the literature data with TAN concentration (Table 2). Moreover, according to Dinuccio et al. [46], no N20 emissions were considered after field application, whereas NH3 losses were calculated as fraction of the Ntot spread into the field for each separated fractions. Finally, NO3 leaching was quantified using the default param- eters recommended by IPCC 2006 [37] for manure management systems. 
An adapted method (Method B) based on Amon's [27,28,45] experimental measurements from raw digested slurry. Simi- larly to Method A, the NH3 and N20 losses during the storage phase, were estimated by scaling the unionized ammonia and TAN concentrations respectively in both summer and winter conditions. In Amon, the slurry tanks are covered with straw or wooden lid, therefore NH3 and N20 emissions during storage will be lower if compared to open tanks. Nevertheless, this is a borderline case which can be useful to evaluate how and to what extent the use of covered tanks for storage of digestate can contribute to decrease nitrogen emissions. As regards spreading, the experimental data about NH3 and N20 emissions per hectare were used, according to the application rate given by the author [45]. Finally, leaching was quantified using the default IPCC 2006 [37] emission factor for manure management systems. 
Table 6 shows the results of nitrogen emissions during digestate storage and agronomic use for each method.</list>

Indirect N20 emissions occur through two pathways (IPCC, 2006): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in digestate, followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO-N per kg N applied). In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and pro- duce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilisation and leaching/runoff respectively. 
EMEP/EEA (2013) Tier 2 approach [42], using the emission fac- tors for cattle manure and slurry. Since the mass balance approach has been applied, not only the NH3 and NOx emissions accounted under the EMEP/EEA method were estimated, but also all the N-containing species lost during storage. More pre- cisely, the estimated emissions include also N20 in air and leaching emissions of NO3 from the solid fraction (both in compliance with the IPCC Tier 1 methodology). For the phase of digestate spreading, the Tier 2 methodology is not based on complete mass balances and gives indications on how to compute only NH3 and NO emissions from soils. In order to complete the LCI data regarding N emissions, IPCC 2006 Tier 1 methodology was applied for estimation of N20 and NO3 emissions. Calculation of NH3 emissions during storage of digestate was based on the TAN content of digestate and the default Tier 2 emission EMEP/EEA factor regarding management of liquid and solid manure from cattle slurry (0.20 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.27 kg N-NH3/kg TAN respectively). Direct N20 emissions for storage were calculated considering the default Tier 1 emission factors of IPCC 2006 for slurry with natural crust</list>

<list>and solid manure, both related to TAN (0.008 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.016 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emis- sions were calculated as fractions of NH3 and NO3 emission (as in Tier 1 IPCC approach). As regards NO emissions, the default EMEP/EEA emission factors (0.0001 kg N-NO/kg TAN and 0.01 kg N-NO/kg TAN for storage of liquid and solid manure respectively) have been used. NO3 leaching from the storage of solid digestate were not included as emissions lost from the system, as it is assumed that leaching from the solid storage is recycled back in the digester plant. As regards spreading, NH3 emissions were estimated by using the default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 emission factors (0.79 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.55 kg N-NH3/kg TAN) regarding spreading of cattle manure and slurry respec- tively. Direct N20 emissions were based on the default Tier 1 emission factor of IPCC 2006 for cattle manure and slurry related to TAN (0.087 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.018 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emissions were calculated as frac- tions from NH3 and NO3 emission following the Tier 1 of the IPCC approach. For NO emissions, EMEP/EEA default Tier 1 emission factors were applied (0.026 kg N-NO/kg TAN for both slurry and manure). For NO3 leaching, the Tier 1 emission factor from IPCC 2006 was applied (0.3 kg N-N03/kg N). 
An adapted method (Method A) using the data reported by Dinuccio et al. [46], where the gaseous emissions from the solid and liquid fractions of the digestate during the storage and spreading phases were measured experimentally both in sum- mer and winter conditions. The amount of gaseous emission of ammonia into atmosphere depends on the gas-liquid phase equilibrium of ammonia nitrogen (NH4 NH3 + H+), where the ratio between unionized ammonia (NH3) and ammonium (NH4) is strictly linked to pH, temperature and ionic strength. Therefore, similarly to the approach reported by Boulamanti et al. [5], the ammonia losses during storage were estimated by scaling the literature data according to the unionized ammonia calculated with the formula given by Béline et al. [47], whereas N20 emissions were estimated by scaling the literature data with TAN concentration (Table 2). Moreover, according to Dinuccio et al. [46], no N20 emissions were considered after field application, whereas NH3 losses were calculated as fraction of the Ntot spread into the field for each separated fractions. Finally, NO3 leaching was quantified using the default param- eters recommended by IPCC 2006 [37] for manure management systems. 
An adapted method (Method B) based on Amon's [27,28,45] experimental measurements from raw digested slurry. Simi- larly to Method A, the NH3 and N20 losses during the storage phase, were estimated by scaling the unionized ammonia and TAN concentrations respectively in both summer and winter conditions. In Amon, the slurry tanks are covered with straw or wooden lid, therefore NH3 and N20 emissions during storage will be lower if compared to open tanks. Nevertheless, this is a borderline case which can be useful to evaluate how and to what extent the use of covered tanks for storage of digestate can contribute to decrease nitrogen emissions. As regards spreading, the experimental data about NH3 and N20 emissions per hectare were used, according to the application rate given by the author [45]. Finally, leaching was quantified using the default IPCC 2006 [37] emission factor for manure management systems. 
Table 6 shows the results of nitrogen emissions during digestate storage and agronomic use for each method.</list>
29: <header>4. Methane and nitrogen emissions due to digestate storage and agronomic use </header>

The residual digestate, due to its high nutrient content, can be used as a fertiliser on agricultural fields, although this can lead to nitrogen airborne and waterborne emissions during storage and field application [40]. Moreover, the anaerobic digestion process continues during digestate storage, and methane emissions can occur [14]. In LCA studies, the measurement of these emissions is difficult and not usually viable within the available budget and time constraints. Therefore, these emissions shall be estimated using models, though no scientific consensus on what models should be adopted currently exists. Therefore, a literature survey was carried out, analysing how published LCA studies on biogas production dealt with the calculation of these emissions.

Some authors do not include the digestate in the system boundaries [17] or do not provide information on how they calculated nitrogen and methane emissions from the management of digestate [16,25]. The experimental data by Amon et al. [27,28], rescaled according to the Volatile Solid (VS) and nitrogen content of the feedstock were used by Boulamanti et al. [5]. Dressler et al. [41] adopt IPCC (2006) [37], whereas Battini et al. [38] utilise a combi- nation of Amon et al. [27,28], IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) [42] models, assimilating the digestate to manure. A combination of IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) were used by Fuchsz and Kohl- heb [10] whereas some papers adopt a combination of IPCC (2006) and other models or national inventories on ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions [15,19-21]. Finally, the models of Nemecek and Kägi [36] were used by De Meester et al. [18] and a combination of Brentrup et al. [43] and Rossier [44] were used by Lijó et al. [11,12].

From the survey it is clear that the most used models in LCA studies are those recommended by IPCC 2006 guidelines Tier 1 or 2 [37]. However, both these models and those in EMEP/EEA [42] have been developed to calculate emissions from the storage and use of organic fertilisers such as animal manure or compost. Moreover, both IPCC and EMEP/EEA models are based on statistical analyses with simplistic assumptions and they do not take explicitly into account all the relevant factors causing the emissions (pH, tem- perature, etc.). Anyway, some papers use data obtained by experi- mental measures. More in particular, Amon [27,28,45] measured N- emissions from not separated digestate (stored in a tank covered by wooden lid or straw) obtained from cattle slurry in an Austrian plant. Finally, Dinuccio et al. [46] measured N emissions from storage (in an open tank) and spreading of separated digestate obtained by an Italian AD plant using a mixture of energy crops, agricultural residues and cow slurry as input materials.

Tier 2 IPCC 2006 [37] approach for manure management, which is an international recognized method widely used by several au- thors as seen above, was adopted in the present study for the calculation of methane emissions due to the storage of digestate. Moreover, Tier 2 is a detailed approach based on specific primary data of the digestate. In fact, the calculation was performed considering primary data about the residual methane potential production of the raw (i.e. not separated) digested material (26.6
Nm³ per ton of VS) and by weighting it on the basis of the amount of volatile matter stored per year for each separated fractions (Table 2).

N20 and NH3 airborne emissions as well as NO3 waterborne emissions from digestate management were calculated with two methods proposed by technical guidelines and two experimental methods, and their effect on the final results was assessed with a sensitivity analysis. In particular the methods were:

<list>IPCC (2006) Tier 1 approach [37], using the default emission factors for cattle manure management, which was adopted for the base case. For the phase of digestate spreading, the Tier 2 methodology is not based on complete mass balances and gives indications on how to compute only NH3 and NO emissions from soils. In order to complete the LCI data regarding N emissions, IPCC 2006 Tier 1 methodology was applied for estimation of N20 and NO3 emissions. Calculation of NH3 emissions during storage of digestate was based on the TAN content of digestate and the default Tier 2 emission EMEP/EEA factor regarding management of liquid and solid manure from cattle slurry (0.20 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.27 kg N-NH3/kg TAN respectively). Direct N20 emissions for storage were calculated considering the default Tier 1 emission factors of IPCC 2006 for slurry with natural crust</list>

<list>and solid manure, both related to TAN (0.008 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.016 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emis- sions were calculated as fractions of NH3 and NO3 emission (as in Tier 1 IPCC approach). As regards NO emissions, the default EMEP/EEA emission factors (0.0001 kg N-NO/kg TAN and 0.01 kg N-NO/kg TAN for storage of liquid and solid manure respectively) have been used. NO3 leaching from the storage of solid digestate were not included as emissions lost from the system, as it is assumed that leaching from the solid storage is recycled back in the digester plant. As regards spreading, NH3 emissions were estimated by using the default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 emission factors (0.79 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.55 kg N-NH3/kg TAN) regarding spreading of cattle manure and slurry respec- tively. Direct N20 emissions were based on the default Tier 1 emission factor of IPCC 2006 for cattle manure and slurry related to TAN (0.087 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.018 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). Indirect N20 emissions were calculated as frac- tions from NH3 and NO3 emission following the Tier 1 of the IPCC approach. For NO emissions, EMEP/EEA default Tier 1 emission factors were applied (0.026 kg N-NO/kg TAN for both slurry and manure). For NO3 leaching, the Tier 1 emission factor from IPCC 2006 was applied (0.3 kg N-N03/kg N). 
An adapted method (Method A) using the data reported by Dinuccio et al. [46], where the gaseous emissions from the solid and liquid fractions of the digestate during the storage and spreading phases were measured experimentally both in sum- mer and winter conditions. The amount of gaseous emission of ammonia into atmosphere depends on the gas-liquid phase equilibrium of ammonia nitrogen (NH4 NH3 + H+), where the ratio between unionized ammonia (NH3) and ammonium (NH4) is strictly linked to pH, temperature and ionic strength. Therefore, similarly to the approach reported by Boulamanti et al. [5], the ammonia losses during storage were estimated by scaling the literature data according to the unionized ammonia calculated with the formula given by Béline et al. [47], whereas N20 emissions were estimated by scaling the literature data with TAN concentration (Table 2). Moreover, according to Dinuccio et al. [46], no N20 emissions were considered after field application, whereas NH3 losses were calculated as fraction of the Ntot spread into the field for each separated fractions. Finally, NO3 leaching was quantified using the default param- eters recommended by IPCC 2006 [37] for manure management systems. 
An adapted method (Method B) based on Amon's [27,28,45] experimental measurements from raw digested slurry. Simi- larly to Method A, the NH3 and N20 losses during the storage phase, were estimated by scaling the unionized ammonia and TAN concentrations respectively in both summer and winter conditions. In Amon, the slurry tanks are covered with straw or wooden lid, therefore NH3 and N20 emissions during storage will be lower if compared to open tanks. Nevertheless, this is a borderline case which can be useful to evaluate how and to what extent the use of covered tanks for storage of digestate can contribute to decrease nitrogen emissions. As regards spreading, the experimental data about NH3 and N20 emissions per hectare were used, according to the application rate given by the author [45]. Finally, leaching was quantified using the default IPCC 2006 [37] emission factor for manure management systems. 
Table 6 shows the results of nitrogen emissions during digestate storage and agronomic use for each method.</list>
32: <header>4. Methane and nitrogen emissions due to digestate storage and agronomic use </header>

The residual digestate, due to its high nutrient content, can be used as a fertiliser on agricultural fields, although this can lead to nitrogen airborne and waterborne emissions during storage and field application [40]. Moreover, the anaerobic digestion process continues during digestate storage, and methane emissions can occur [14]. In LCA studies, the measurement of these emissions is difficult and not usually viable within the available budget and time constraints. Therefore, these emissions shall be estimated using models, though no scientific consensus on what models should be adopted currently exists. Therefore, a literature survey was carried out, analysing how published LCA studies on biogas production dealt with the calculation of these emissions.

Some authors do not include the digestate in the system boundaries [17] or do not provide information on how they calculated nitrogen and methane emissions from the management of digestate [16,25]. The experimental data by Amon et al. [27,28], rescaled according to the Volatile Solid (VS) and nitrogen content of the feedstock were used by Boulamanti et al. [5]. Dressler et al. [41] adopt IPCC (2006) [37], whereas Battini et al. [38] utilise a combi- nation of Amon et al. [27,28], IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) [42] models, assimilating the digestate to manure. A combination of IPCC (2006) and EMEP/EEA (2013) were used by Fuchsz and Kohl- heb [10] whereas some papers adopt a combination of IPCC (2006) and other models or national inventories on ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions [15,19-21]. Finally, the models of Nemecek and Kägi [36] were used by De Meester et al. [18] and a combination of Brentrup et al. [43] and Rossier [44] were used by Lijó et al. [11,12].

From the survey it is clear that the most used models in LCA studies are those recommended by IPCC 2006 guidelines Tier 1 or 2 [37]. However, both these models and those in EMEP/EEA [42] have been developed to calculate emissions from the storage and use of organic fertilisers such as animal manure or compost. Moreover, both IPCC and EMEP/EEA models are based on statistical analyses with simplistic assumptions and they do not take explicitly into account all the relevant factors causing the emissions (pH, tem- perature, etc.). Anyway, some papers use data obtained by experi- mental measures. More in particular, Amon [27,28,45] measured N- emissions from not separated digestate (stored in a tank covered by wooden lid or straw) obtained from cattle slurry in an Austrian plant. Finally, Dinuccio et al. [46] measured N emissions from storage (in an open tank) and spreading of separated digestate obtained by an Italian AD plant using a mixture of energy crops, agricultural residues and cow slurry as input materials.

Tier 2 IPCC 2006 [37] approach for manure management, which is an international recognized method widely used by several au- thors as seen above, was adopted in the present study for the calculation of methane emissions due to the storage of digestate. Moreover, Tier 2 is a detailed approach based on specific primary data of the digestate. In fact, the calculation was performed considering primary data about the residual methane potential production of the raw (i.e. not separated) digested material (26.6
Nm³ per ton of VS) and by weighting it on the basis of the amount of volatile matter stored per year for each separated fractions (Table 2).

N20 and NH3 airborne emissions as well as NO3 waterborne emissions from digestate management were calculated with two methods proposed by technical guidelines and two experimental methods, and their effect on the final results was assessed with a sensitivity analysis. In particular the methods were:

<list>IPCC (2006) Tier 1 approach [37], using the default emission factors for cattle manure management, which was adopted for the base case. Direct N20 airborne emissions occurring during the storage of digestate are produced by nitrification and deni- trification of nitrogen contained in digestate. Calculation was based on the total amount of nitrogen contained in liquid and solid digestate and the emission factor regarding manure management system (0.005 kg N20-N per kg N for liquid manure stored in open tanks and 0.005 kg N20-N per kg N for solid manure stored in unconfined piles). Indirect N20 emissions from the volatilisation of digestate during storage were calcu- lated assuming that 40% of the digestate nitrogen is volatilised as NH3 and NOx and multiplying this value by the emission factor for N20 emissions from atmospheric deposition of ni- trogen on soils and water surfaces (0.01 kg N20-N per kg NH3-N volatilized). NH3-N emissions were estimated equal to 40% of the nitrogen content in the digestate, since IPCC approach does not provide sufficient information on how to separate NH3 and NO emissions. Direct N20 emissions resulting from the application to soils of nitrogen contained in digestate were calculated considering that 1% of the total nitrogen applied is released as N20. Nitrogen available for spreading was calculated by subtracting from the total nitrogen content of the digestate the nitrogen emitted as N20 and NH3 during storage. Indirect N20 emissions occur through two pathways (IPCC, 2006): (1) volatilization of nitrogen as NH3 and NOx, which was estimated equal to 20% of the amount of nitrogen contained in digestate, followed by deposition of nitrogen onto soil/water; (2) leaching and runoff of nitrogen (0.3 kg NO-N per kg N applied). In both pathways initrification/denitrification reactions follow and pro- duce N20 emissions, which can be estimated as 0.01 kg N20-N emitted per kg N volatilized and 0.0075 kg N20-N per kg N leaching/runoff. NH3 airborne emissions and NO3 waterborne emissions were quantified in agreement with the above mentioned rates of volatilisation and leaching/runoff respectively. 
EMEP/EEA (2013) Tier 2 approach [42], using the emission fac- tors for cattle manure and slurry. Since the mass balance approach has been applied, not only the NH3 and NOx emissions accounted under the EMEP/EEA method were estimated, but also all the N-containing species lost during storage. More pre- cisely, the estimated emissions include also N20 in air and leaching emissions of NO3 from the solid fraction (both in compliance with the IPCC Tier 1 methodology). For the phase of digestate spreading, the Tier 2 methodology is not based on complete mass balances and gives indications on how to compute only NH3 and NO emissions from soils. In order to complete the LCI data regarding N emissions, IPCC 2006 Tier 1 methodology was applied for estimation of N20 and NO3 emissions. <list>and solid manure, both related to TAN (0.008 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.016 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). As regards NO emissions, the default EMEP/EEA emission factors (0.0001 kg N-NO/kg TAN and 0.01 kg N-NO/kg TAN for storage of liquid and solid manure respectively) have been used. NO3 leaching from the storage of solid digestate were not included as emissions lost from the system, as it is assumed that leaching from the solid storage is recycled back in the digester plant. As regards spreading, NH3 emissions were estimated by using the default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 emission factors (0.79 kg N-NH3/kg TAN and 0.55 kg N-NH3/kg TAN) regarding spreading of cattle manure and slurry respec- tively. Direct N20 emissions were based on the default Tier 1 emission factor of IPCC 2006 for cattle manure and slurry related to TAN (0.087 kg N-N20/kg TAN and 0.018 kg N-N20/kg TAN respectively). The life cycle of the production of 1 MJ of electricity was divided in four main phases: 1) biomass cultivation at farm; 2) AD plant; 3) transport of biomass and residues to the AD plant and transport of digestate to farms; 4) storage and spreading of digestate on agri- cultural fields. Biomass production has significant potential impacts in OD and F-EU. Emissions from digestate storage and spreading are the main hotspots for GW, AC and M-EU. AD plant shows remark- able contribution in POF and MFRD.
37: The emissions of N20, CO2 and CH4 account for 42%, 39% and 17% respectively of total Global Warming potential impact. The contri- bution of biomass production phase to total GHG emissions is due to CO2 (60%), N20 (37%) and CH4 (3%), which are mainly emitted from the production and use of chemical fertilisers and diesel consumption during agricultural work processes. The contribution of AD plant is equal to 0.022 kg CO2 eq.: the 61% is due to CH4 emissions from the methane leakages and the 37% is due to CO2 emissions from the diesel consumption for input material opera- tions (i.e. handling and pressing) and from the production of building materials for infrastructures. Transports show minor contribution, mainly due to CO2 emissions. The contribution of digestate to total GW is due to N20 emissions (83%) from the storage and spreading of digestate, CO2 emissions (10%) from the diesel consumption for agricultural machinery used to spread the digestate on fields and CH4 (6%) from digestate storage. In partic- ular, N20 emissions from digestate phase are 0.023 kg CO2 eq.: the 52% are due to storage of liquid and solid digestate, whereas the
48% are due to spreading. The higher N20 emissions during storage are due to the high nitrogen content of separated digestate.

39: <header>5.2. Ozone Depletion and Photochemical Ozone Formation </header>

As regards OD, the major emissions in all phases of the life cycle are Halon 1301 and Halon 1211, which account for 72% and 20% respectively of total OD potential. In all phases, these emissions are mainly due to diesel and electricity production. The contribution of biomass production to total POF is due to NOx (84%) and NMVOC (13%) emissions, mainly from diesel consumption, production of chemical fertilisers and agricultural machinery. AD plant contrib- utes 2.7E-03 kg NMVOC eq. to this impact category: the 79% is due to NOx and the 10% is due to CO emissions from biogas combustion, diesel and electricity consumption. The contribution of transport is mainly due to NO and NMVOC emissions. The contribution of digestate management is due to NO (81%) and NMVOC (16%), both from diesel consumption.

Globally, NH3 and NO airborne emissions are responsible for 94% and 4% of AC. Biomass production phase is responsible for 10% of total AC, because of NH3 (82%) and NO (11%) emissions mainly from production and use of chemical fertilisers. AD plant and transports show minor contributions, mainly due to biogas com- bustion in the CHP plant and diesel consumption. Digestate man- agement contributes 5.4E-03 mol H+ eq. (87% of total) to this impact category, which is almost completely due to NH3 emissions

42: The contribution of biomass production to total F-EU is completely due to phosphate waterborne emissions (phosphorus is the limiting factor in F-EU) from the production and use of chemical fertilisers. AD plant is responsible for 25% of this impact category: phosphate emissions contribute 100% of total emissions mainly derived from the production of plant infrastructures. Phosphate emissions in transport and digestate phases are mainly due to the production of agricultural machinery and direct field emissions from the spreading of digestate.
As regards M-EU (where nitrogen is the limiting factor), the emissions of NO3, NH3 and NO account for 74%, 14% and 11%

respectively of the total impact category. The contribution of biomass production is mainly derived from NO3 (89%) because of field emissions from chemical fertilisers. AD plant and transports show minor contributions, almost completely due to NOx emissions from the combustion of biogas and diesel. Digestate phase is the main hotspot for this impact category: the 75% and 24% respectively are due to NO3 waterborne emissions and NH3 airborne emissions from the storage and spreading of digestate.

46: <header>5.6. Uncertainties propagation by Monte Carlo method </header>

In general, according to the Gauss and Laplace teories, the propagation of uncertainties expresses to what extent the un- certainties of final results are affected by the uncertainties of input data. Therefore, the propagation of uncertainties should be a fundamental feature of LCA method because, with data

transparency and scientific consistency of the results, it allows improving the reliability of an LCA study. Nevertheless, un- certainties propagation is seldom considered by both the interna- tional guides and the majority of the authors. The uncertainties propagation can be performed by means of the following methods:

<list>1. Analytical method by means of Taylor expansion; 
2. Stochastic sampling by means of Monte Carlo algorithm.</list>

Although providing well-reproducible results, the former is quite difficult to perform because of the mathematical background needed. The latter is theoretically difficult as well, but it can be used as an algorithm implemented in LCA commercial softwares. In fact, it allows considering many probability distributions and calculating several statistical parameters like mean value, variance, standard
kg CFC-11e
molc eq
Fig. 7. Lognormal shaped limit distribution for Acidification.
deviation, coefficient of variation, etc (more than those provided from the analytical method).

As regards LCA studies, Monte Carlo method propagates the uncertainties from the input parameters (background data un- certainties, if available) to the output parameters (the final results), thus providing a well-known probability distribution for the result in each impact category [48-52]. The final result provided by the Monte Carlo calculation is represented by a graph (frequency his- togram) showing the obtained limit distribution with its localiza- tion parameter and dispersion parameter.

The Monte Carlo analysis of the LCA results for this study was performed using the Monte Carlo module of SimaPro 7.3.3 software on background data (Ecoinvent v2.0 flows). The number of itera- tions was set on 5000.

Ecoinvent v.2.0 has a coherent system for dealing with un- certainties: on a level of unit process and for almost all data points (i.e. for each input or output from a process unit), the standard probability distribution is assumed to be the lognormal distribu- tion, with a confidence level of 95% [53].
Four probability distributions are coded in the Monte Carlo module of the SimaPro software:

<list>1. Uniform; 
2. Triangular; 
3. Normal; 
4. Lognormal.</list>
The final results from SimaPro can be represented, with a

Of the mean Global Warming|kg CO2 eq.|8.07E-02|8.06E-02|9.14E-04|1.13%|7.90E-02|8.25E-02|1.60E-04 Ozone Depletion|kg CFC-11 eq.|3.85E-09|3.70E-09|9.15E-10|23.80%|2.48E-09|6.08E-09|3.37E-03 Photochemical Ozone Formation|kg NMVOC eq.|4.55E-04|4.55E-04|1.29E-05|2.84%|4.32E-04|4.83E-04|4.02E-04 Acidification|mol H+ eq.|6.24E-03|6.24E-03|1.39E-05|0.22%|6.22E-03|6.27E-03|3.15E-05 Freshwater Eutrophication|kg P eq.|1.12E-05|1.06E-05|3.11E-06|27.70%|7.52E-06|1.89E-05|3.92E-03 Marine Eutrophication|kg N eq.|1.24E-03|1.24E-03|6.84E-06|0.55%|1.23E-03|1.26E-03|7.79E-05 Mineral, fossil & ren resource depletion|kg Sb eq.|9.55E-07|8.28E-07|1.06E-06|111%|-7.69E-07|3.37E-06|1.57E-02
Fig. 11. Uncertainty of impact categories in terms of coefficient of variation.
confidence level of 95%, as a bar-graph showing the error-bar for each impact category or as a frequency histogram showing the obtained limit distribution with the following parameters:

<list>Mean value: arithmetic mean of the values; 
Median: abscissa of the function that splits the function itself in two equal parts; 
Standard deviation: the SimaPro Monte Carlo module calculates the normal standard deviation whatever the obtained limit distribution is (e.g. if the limit distribution is a lognormal, the module does not calculate the geometric standard deviation); 
Coefficient of variation: the ratio between the standard devia- tion and the mean is an index of dispersion that allows comparing measures related to different units of measurement, since it is a pure number (i.e. not reported to any unit of mea- sure). It is an index of the precision of a measurement; 
Standard error of the mean: the amount by which the last calculation influences the mean; it is the stop criterion equiva- lent to the specification of the number of calculation cycles (number of runs).</list>

Therefore the uncertainty of impact categories can be repre- sented in terms of coefficient of variation. The variability of the data, represented by the standard deviation, is expressed in units of arithmetic mean in order to obtain a pure number that allows the comparison between quantities expressed in different units of measure. In other words, the coefficient of variation allows to evaluate the dispersion of the measurements around the mean, independently from the unit of measure of the quantity considered.

The analysis of the Monte Carlo calculation shows the final uncertainties for each impact category. Figs. 3-5 show the gaussian-like trend for the probability distribution of GW, POF and M-EU impact category.
Figs. 6-9 show the lognormal-like trend for the probability distribution of OD, AC, MFRD and F-EU impact categories.

Fig. 10 shows the impact assessment profile of 1 MJ of electriticy with confidence interval. The uncertainty-ranges of the bar-chart expresses the 95% confidence interval, i.e. the 95% of the results are located inside this range. OD, F-EU and MFRD results have the
largest uncertainty-ranges impact categories, i.e. these impact categories present the highest data variability.

Table 8 shows the numerical results of the Monte Carlo calcu- lation in terms of statistical characteristics related to probability distribution of each impact category (see Figs. 3-9): mean, median, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, standard error of the mean.

The bars in Fig. 10 have the same height because quantities expressed in different units of measurements cannot be compared. Therefore the uncertainties can be expressed in terms of coefficient of variation in order to compare the results (Fig. 11 and Table 8). Fig. 11 shows that OD, F-EU and MFRD have the highest values of the coefficient of variation.

In conclusion, the highest uncertainty is associated to the cat- egories MFRD, F-EU and OD whereas the lowest uncertainty is associated to the categories AC, M-EU, GW and POF (Figs. 10 and 11). These trends are probably due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent background data, propagating to the final results.

The Monte Carlo analysis of the LCA results for this study was performed using the Monte Carlo module of SimaPro 7.3.3 software on background data (Ecoinvent v2.0 flows). The number of itera- tions was set on 5000.

Ecoinvent v.2.0 has a coherent system for dealing with un- certainties: on a level of unit process and for almost all data points (i.e. for each input or output from a process unit), the standard probability distribution is assumed to be the lognormal distribu- tion, with a confidence level of 95% [53].
Four probability distributions are coded in the Monte Carlo module of the SimaPro software:

<list>1. Uniform; 
2. Triangular; 
3. Normal; 
4. Lognormal.</list>

Of the mean Global Warming|kg CO2 eq.|8.07E-02|8.06E-02|9.14E-04|1.13%|7.90E-02|8.25E-02|1.60E-04 Ozone Depletion|kg CFC-11 eq.|3.85E-09|3.70E-09|9.15E-10|23.80%|2.48E-09|6.08E-09|3.37E-03 Photochemical Ozone Formation|kg NMVOC eq.|4.55E-04|4.55E-04|1.29E-05|2.84%|4.32E-04|4.83E-04|4.02E-04 Acidification|mol H+ eq.|6.24E-03|6.24E-03|1.39E-05|0.22%|6.22E-03|6.27E-03|3.15E-05 Freshwater Eutrophication|kg P eq.|1.12E-05|1.06E-05|3.11E-06|27.70%|7.52E-06|1.89E-05|3.92E-03 Marine Eutrophication|kg N eq.|1.24E-03|1.24E-03|6.84E-06|0.55%|1.23E-03|1.26E-03|7.79E-05 Mineral, fossil & ren resource depletion|kg Sb eq.|9.55E-07|8.28E-07|1.06E-06|111%|-7.69E-07|3.37E-06|1.57E-02
Fig. 11. Uncertainty of impact categories in terms of coefficient of variation.
confidence level of 95%, as a bar-graph showing the error-bar for each impact category or as a frequency histogram showing the obtained limit distribution with the following parameters:

<list>Mean value: arithmetic mean of the values; 
Median: abscissa of the function that splits the function itself in two equal parts; 
Standard deviation: the SimaPro Monte Carlo module calculates the normal standard deviation whatever the obtained limit distribution is (e.g. if the limit distribution is a lognormal, the module does not calculate the geometric standard deviation); 
Coefficient of variation: the ratio between the standard devia- tion and the mean is an index of dispersion that allows comparing measures related to different units of measurement, since it is a pure number (i.e. not reported to any unit of mea- sure). It is an index of the precision of a measurement; 
Standard error of the mean: the amount by which the last calculation influences the mean; it is the stop criterion equiva- lent to the specification of the number of calculation cycles (number of runs).</list>

Therefore the uncertainty of impact categories can be repre- sented in terms of coefficient of variation. The variability of the data, represented by the standard deviation, is expressed in units of arithmetic mean in order to obtain a pure number that allows the comparison between quantities expressed in different units of measure. In other words, the coefficient of variation allows to evaluate the dispersion of the measurements around the mean, independently from the unit of measure of the quantity considered.

The analysis of the Monte Carlo calculation shows the final uncertainties for each impact category. Figs. 3-5 show the gaussian-like trend for the probability distribution of GW, POF and M-EU impact category.
Figs. 6-9 show the lognormal-like trend for the probability distribution of OD, AC, MFRD and F-EU impact categories.

Fig. 10 shows the impact assessment profile of 1 MJ of electriticy with confidence interval. The uncertainty-ranges of the bar-chart expresses the 95% confidence interval, i.e. the 95% of the results are located inside this range. OD, F-EU and MFRD results have the
largest uncertainty-ranges impact categories, i.e. these impact categories present the highest data variability.

Table 8 shows the numerical results of the Monte Carlo calcu- lation in terms of statistical characteristics related to probability distribution of each impact category (see Figs. 3-9): mean, median, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, standard error of the mean.

The bars in Fig. 10 have the same height because quantities expressed in different units of measurements cannot be compared. Therefore the uncertainties can be expressed in terms of coefficient of variation in order to compare the results (Fig. 11 and Table 8). Fig. 11 shows that OD, F-EU and MFRD have the highest values of the coefficient of variation.

In conclusion, the highest uncertainty is associated to the cat- egories MFRD, F-EU and OD whereas the lowest uncertainty is associated to the categories AC, M-EU, GW and POF (Figs. 10 and 11). These trends are probably due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent background data, propagating to the final results.
49: <header>5.6. Uncertainties propagation by Monte Carlo method </header>

In general, according to the Gauss and Laplace teories, the propagation of uncertainties expresses to what extent the un- certainties of final results are affected by the uncertainties of input data. Therefore, the propagation of uncertainties should be a fundamental feature of LCA method because, with data

transparency and scientific consistency of the results, it allows improving the reliability of an LCA study. Nevertheless, un- certainties propagation is seldom considered by both the interna- tional guides and the majority of the authors. The uncertainties propagation can be performed by means of the following methods:

<list>1. Analytical method by means of Taylor expansion; 
2. Stochastic sampling by means of Monte Carlo algorithm.</list>

Although providing well-reproducible results, the former is quite difficult to perform because of the mathematical background needed. The latter is theoretically difficult as well, but it can be used as an algorithm implemented in LCA commercial softwares. In fact, it allows considering many probability distributions and calculating several statistical parameters like mean value, variance, standard
kg CFC-11e
molc eq

deviation, coefficient of variation, etc (more than those provided from the analytical method).
