Of the mean Global Warming|kg CO2 eq.|8.07E-02|8.06E-02|9.14E-04|1.13%|7.90E-02|8.25E-02|1.60E-04 Ozone Depletion|kg CFC-11 eq.|3.85E-09|3.70E-09|9.15E-10|23.80%|2.48E-09|6.08E-09|3.37E-03 Photochemical Ozone Formation|kg NMVOC eq.|4.55E-04|4.55E-04|1.29E-05|2.84%|4.32E-04|4.83E-04|4.02E-04 Acidification|mol H+ eq.|6.24E-03|6.24E-03|1.39E-05|0.22%|6.22E-03|6.27E-03|3.15E-05 Freshwater Eutrophication|kg P eq.|1.12E-05|1.06E-05|3.11E-06|27.70%|7.52E-06|1.89E-05|3.92E-03 Marine Eutrophication|kg N eq.|1.24E-03|1.24E-03|6.84E-06|0.55%|1.23E-03|1.26E-03|7.79E-05 Mineral, fossil & ren resource depletion|kg Sb eq.|9.55E-07|8.28E-07|1.06E-06|111%|-7.69E-07|3.37E-06|1.57E-02
Fig. 11. Uncertainty of impact categories in terms of coefficient of variation.
confidence level of 95%, as a bar-graph showing the error-bar for each impact category or as a frequency histogram showing the obtained limit distribution with the following parameters:

<list>Mean value: arithmetic mean of the values; 
Median: abscissa of the function that splits the function itself in two equal parts; 
Standard deviation: the SimaPro Monte Carlo module calculates the normal standard deviation whatever the obtained limit distribution is (e.g. if the limit distribution is a lognormal, the module does not calculate the geometric standard deviation); 
Coefficient of variation: the ratio between the standard devia- tion and the mean is an index of dispersion that allows comparing measures related to different units of measurement, since it is a pure number (i.e. not reported to any unit of mea- sure). It is an index of the precision of a measurement; 
Standard error of the mean: the amount by which the last calculation influences the mean; it is the stop criterion equiva- lent to the specification of the number of calculation cycles (number of runs).</list>

Therefore the uncertainty of impact categories can be repre- sented in terms of coefficient of variation. The variability of the data, represented by the standard deviation, is expressed in units of arithmetic mean in order to obtain a pure number that allows the comparison between quantities expressed in different units of measure. In other words, the coefficient of variation allows to evaluate the dispersion of the measurements around the mean, independently from the unit of measure of the quantity considered.

The analysis of the Monte Carlo calculation shows the final uncertainties for each impact category. Figs. 3-5 show the gaussian-like trend for the probability distribution of GW, POF and M-EU impact category.
Figs. 6-9 show the lognormal-like trend for the probability distribution of OD, AC, MFRD and F-EU impact categories.

Fig. 10 shows the impact assessment profile of 1 MJ of electriticy with confidence interval. The uncertainty-ranges of the bar-chart expresses the 95% confidence interval, i.e. the 95% of the results are located inside this range. OD, F-EU and MFRD results have the
largest uncertainty-ranges impact categories, i.e. these impact categories present the highest data variability.

Table 8 shows the numerical results of the Monte Carlo calcu- lation in terms of statistical characteristics related to probability distribution of each impact category (see Figs. 3-9): mean, median, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, standard error of the mean.

The bars in Fig. 10 have the same height because quantities expressed in different units of measurements cannot be compared. Therefore the uncertainties can be expressed in terms of coefficient of variation in order to compare the results (Fig. 11 and Table 8). Fig. 11 shows that OD, F-EU and MFRD have the highest values of the coefficient of variation.

In conclusion, the highest uncertainty is associated to the cat- egories MFRD, F-EU and OD whereas the lowest uncertainty is associated to the categories AC, M-EU, GW and POF (Figs. 10 and 11). These trends are probably due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent background data, propagating to the final results.
53: <header>5.6. Uncertainties propagation by Monte Carlo method </header>

In general, according to the Gauss and Laplace teories, the propagation of uncertainties expresses to what extent the un- certainties of final results are affected by the uncertainties of input data. Therefore, the propagation of uncertainties should be a fundamental feature of LCA method because, with data

transparency and scientific consistency of the results, it allows improving the reliability of an LCA study. Nevertheless, un- certainties propagation is seldom considered by both the interna- tional guides and the majority of the authors. The uncertainties propagation can be performed by means of the following methods:

<list>1. Analytical method by means of Taylor expansion; 
2. Stochastic sampling by means of Monte Carlo algorithm.</list>

Although providing well-reproducible results, the former is quite difficult to perform because of the mathematical background needed. The latter is theoretically difficult as well, but it can be used as an algorithm implemented in LCA commercial softwares. In fact, it allows considering many probability distributions and calculating several statistical parameters like mean value, variance, standard
kg CFC-11e
molc eq
Fig. 7. Lognormal shaped limit distribution for Acidification.
deviation, coefficient of variation, etc (more than those provided from the analytical method).

As regards LCA studies, Monte Carlo method propagates the uncertainties from the input parameters (background data un- certainties, if available) to the output parameters (the final results), thus providing a well-known probability distribution for the result in each impact category [48-52]. The final result provided by the Monte Carlo calculation is represented by a graph (frequency his- togram) showing the obtained limit distribution with its localiza- tion parameter and dispersion parameter.

The Monte Carlo analysis of the LCA results for this study was performed using the Monte Carlo module of SimaPro 7.3.3 software on background data (Ecoinvent v2.0 flows). The number of itera- tions was set on 5000.

Ecoinvent v.2.0 has a coherent system for dealing with un- certainties: on a level of unit process and for almost all data points (i.e. for each input or output from a process unit), the standard probability distribution is assumed to be the lognormal distribu- tion, with a confidence level of 95% [53].
Four probability distributions are coded in the Monte Carlo module of the SimaPro software:

<list>1. Uniform; 
2. Triangular; 
3. Normal; 
4. Lognormal.</list>
The final results from SimaPro can be represented, with a

Coefficient of variation: the ratio between the standard devia- tion and the mean is an index of dispersion that allows comparing measures related to different units of measurement, since it is a pure number (i.e. not reported to any unit of mea- sure). It is an index of the precision of a measurement; 
Standard error of the mean: the amount by which the last calculation influences the mean; it is the stop criterion equiva- lent to the specification of the number of calculation cycles (number of runs).</list>

Therefore the uncertainty of impact categories can be repre- sented in terms of coefficient of variation. The variability of the data, represented by the standard deviation, is expressed in units of arithmetic mean in order to obtain a pure number that allows the comparison between quantities expressed in different units of measure. In other words, the coefficient of variation allows to evaluate the dispersion of the measurements around the mean, independently from the unit of measure of the quantity considered.

The analysis of the Monte Carlo calculation shows the final uncertainties for each impact category. Figs. 3-5 show the gaussian-like trend for the probability distribution of GW, POF and M-EU impact category.
Figs. 6-9 show the lognormal-like trend for the probability distribution of OD, AC, MFRD and F-EU impact categories.

Fig. 10 shows the impact assessment profile of 1 MJ of electriticy with confidence interval. The uncertainty-ranges of the bar-chart expresses the 95% confidence interval, i.e. the 95% of the results are located inside this range. OD, F-EU and MFRD results have the
largest uncertainty-ranges impact categories, i.e. these impact categories present the highest data variability.

Table 8 shows the numerical results of the Monte Carlo calcu- lation in terms of statistical characteristics related to probability distribution of each impact category (see Figs. 3-9): mean, median, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, standard error of the mean.

The bars in Fig. 10 have the same height because quantities expressed in different units of measurements cannot be compared. Therefore the uncertainties can be expressed in terms of coefficient of variation in order to compare the results (Fig. 11 and Table 8). Fig. 11 shows that OD, F-EU and MFRD have the highest values of the coefficient of variation.

In conclusion, the highest uncertainty is associated to the cat- egories MFRD, F-EU and OD whereas the lowest uncertainty is associated to the categories AC, M-EU, GW and POF (Figs. 10 and 11). These trends are probably due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent background data, propagating to the final results.
57: <header>5.6. Uncertainties propagation by Monte Carlo method </header>

In general, according to the Gauss and Laplace teories, the propagation of uncertainties expresses to what extent the un- certainties of final results are affected by the uncertainties of input data. Therefore, the propagation of uncertainties should be a fundamental feature of LCA method because, with data

transparency and scientific consistency of the results, it allows improving the reliability of an LCA study. Nevertheless, un- certainties propagation is seldom considered by both the interna- tional guides and the majority of the authors. The uncertainties propagation can be performed by means of the following methods:

<list>1. Analytical method by means of Taylor expansion; 
2. Stochastic sampling by means of Monte Carlo algorithm.</list>

Although providing well-reproducible results, the former is quite difficult to perform because of the mathematical background needed. The latter is theoretically difficult as well, but it can be used as an algorithm implemented in LCA commercial softwares. In fact, it allows considering many probability distributions and calculating several statistical parameters like mean value, variance, standard
kg CFC-11e
molc eq
Fig. 7. Lognormal shaped limit distribution for Acidification.
deviation, coefficient of variation, etc (more than those provided from the analytical method).

As regards LCA studies, Monte Carlo method propagates the uncertainties from the input parameters (background data un- certainties, if available) to the output parameters (the final results), thus providing a well-known probability distribution for the result in each impact category [48-52]. The final result provided by the Monte Carlo calculation is represented by a graph (frequency his- togram) showing the obtained limit distribution with its localiza- tion parameter and dispersion parameter.

The Monte Carlo analysis of the LCA results for this study was performed using the Monte Carlo module of SimaPro 7.3.3 software on background data (Ecoinvent v2.0 flows). The number of itera- tions was set on 5000.

Ecoinvent v.2.0 has a coherent system for dealing with un- certainties: on a level of unit process and for almost all data points (i.e. for each input or output from a process unit), the standard probability distribution is assumed to be the lognormal distribu- tion, with a confidence level of 95% [53].
Four probability distributions are coded in the Monte Carlo module of the SimaPro software:

<list>1. Uniform; 
2. Triangular; 
3. Normal; 
4. Lognormal.</list>

60: The sensitivity analysis aims to determine to what extent the results of Global Warming, Acidification and Marine Eutrophication impact categories are affected by the choice of model for calculating nitrogen emissions from the storage and spreading of digestate. The results are presented in Table 9. Table 10 details the main emissions obtained in each impact category.

As regards GW, the results applying EMEP/EEA [42] are 3% higher than the base case, due to the slightly higher N20 emissions from storage and spreading (Table 4); on the contrary, if adopting Method A and Method B, which estimate lower N20 emissions, the values of GW are 29% and 13% lower than the base case respectively. AC values are 25% and 36% lower in EMEP/EEA [42] and Method A respectively than in the base case, due to lower NH3 emissions (Table 4). In addition, AC values of Method B are 77% lower than the base case: the covered storage tank leads to minor NH3 emissions if compared to open storage, which is used in the plant examined. Finally, for M-EU, the values of both EMEP/EEA [42] and Method A are higher than the base case (15% and 17% respectively), because of higher NO3 emissions (Table 4). On the contrary, if adopting Method B, the results of M-EU are 36% lower than the base case results, due to lower NO3 emissions.

62: The wide spread of anaerobic digestion for renewable energy production requires a deep and detailed analysis of the environ- mental benefits and negative effects of that technology. This paper presents an LCA study of an AD plant for electricity production located in Italy which uses energy crops mixed with agricultural residues and slurry from livestock as input materials. The numerical results here presented are strongly dependent from the specific substrate and cannot be extrapolated for the technology in general. Anyway, this study represents the state of the art of LCA in anaer- obic digestion field with the use of sensitivity and uncertainty analysis. As regards the former, four methods were compared for
Table 9 Results of sensitivity analysis referred to 1 MJel.

Impact category|Unit|Base case IPCC|EMEP/EEA|Method A|Method B Global Warming|kg CO2 eq.|8.07E-02|8.30E-02|5.77E-02|7.03E-02 Acidification|mol H+ eq.|6.24E-03|4.66E-03|4.01E-03|1.43E-03 Marine Eutrophication|kg N eq.|1.24E-03|1.43E-03|1.45E-03|8.00E-04
63: Main emissions resulting from the sensitivity analysis in each impact category referred to 1 MJel.

Unit|Base case IPCC|EMEP/EEA|Method A|Method B Global Warming|Global Warming|Global Warming|Global Warming|Global Warming N20 kg CO2 eq|3.30E-02|3.53E-02|1.00E-02|2.32E-02 Acidification|Acidification|Acidification|Acidification|Acidification NH3 mol H+ eq|5.89E-03|4.27E-03|3.66E-03|1.08E-03 Marine Eutrophication|Marine Eutrophication|Marine Eutrophication|Marine Eutrophication|Marine Eutrophication NO- kg N eq|9.20E-04|1.14E-03|1.20E-03|6.26E-04 NH3 kg N eq|1.79E-04|1.30E-04|1.11E-04|3.28E-05

the calculation of emissions from digestate: 1) two models from technical guidelines (IPCC and EMEP/EEA); 2) two methods based on experimental measures. As far as the latter is concerned, it is seldom applied in literature LCA studies but should be used by LCA practitioners to improve the reliability of results. The sensitivity analysis shows that the choice of methods for calculation of emis- sions from digestate is a critical point for practitioners, as it significantly affects the LCA results. In particular, the borderline case based on Amon [27,28,45], who measures emissions from storage of digested slurry contained in covered tanks, is useful to understand to what extent covered tanks can contribute to decreasing nitrogen emissions. The propagation of uncertainties was performed by means of Monte Carlo analysis on Ecoinvent background data and showed the final uncertainties for the result of each impact category, in terms of several statistical parameters. The analysis highlights that MFRD, F-EU and OD have the highest data variability and therefore they have the highest uncertainty, which is due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent data, propagating to the final results. Monte Carlo analysis is therefore a robust method to improve the reliability of the results of this LCA study. Finally, this LCA study allows identifying some general improvement solutions for the environmental profile of anaerobic digestion plants, which could be applied by plant managers for an environmental optimisation and could be useful for future planning of other anaerobic digestion plants. Specific mitigation measures are summarised in the following points.

As far as the latter is concerned, it is seldom applied in literature LCA studies but should be used by LCA practitioners to improve the reliability of results. The sensitivity analysis shows that the choice of methods for calculation of emis- sions from digestate is a critical point for practitioners, as it significantly affects the LCA results. In particular, the borderline case based on Amon [27,28,45], who measures emissions from storage of digested slurry contained in covered tanks, is useful to understand to what extent covered tanks can contribute to decreasing nitrogen emissions. The propagation of uncertainties was performed by means of Monte Carlo analysis on Ecoinvent background data and showed the final uncertainties for the result of each impact category, in terms of several statistical parameters. The analysis highlights that MFRD, F-EU and OD have the highest data variability and therefore they have the highest uncertainty, which is due to high uncertainty values in some Ecoinvent data, propagating to the final results. Monte Carlo analysis is therefore a robust method to improve the reliability of the results of this LCA study. Finally, this LCA study allows identifying some general improvement solutions for the environmental profile of anaerobic digestion plants, which could be applied by plant managers for an environmental optimisation and could be useful for future planning of other anaerobic digestion plants. Specific mitigation measures are summarised in the following points.
65: Biomass production with dedicated crops is responsible for about 33% of the total GW, 50% of the OD, 25% of the POF, more than 35% of the M-EU and F-EU and 20% of MFRD. It is evident that AD plants using only residues would have a much better environ- mental profile. This result confirms that AD is a viable technology for energy recovery from organic waste and residues. The use of limited quantity of dedicated crops should be seen as a means for a better management of the plant and within a more general perspective of crop rotation cultivation.
66: Measurement of nitrogen and methane emissions from diges- tate storage and spreading is not usually viable within an LCA study, therefore the emissions shall be estimated using literature models, though no scientific consensus on what models should be adopted currently exists. The sensitivity analysis showed that the final re- sults on GW, AC and M-EU are strongly affected by the chosen models and methods. Therefore, it is strongly recommended to continue the R&D work in that field. However, the use of a closed tank for storing the digestate (now requested by the Italian regional regulation [54]), either with protective layer or with the use of

flexible storage, can drastically reduce the emissions of NH3 and N20, which are responsible for 85% of the total AC and for 30% of total GW, respectively. The effect of covered storage tanks in decreasing nitrogen emissions can also be seen in the sensitivity analysis.
67: Mitigation of environmental impacts due to the management of the AD plant and the electricity production could be achieved mainly identifying a way for using the heat generated in the CHP, which is currently used only partially for heating the digestion tanks. Moreover, it should be paid attention to ways for reducing the fugitive emissions of CH4 from the AD plant, which is respon- sible for 15% of the total GW of the system.
68: When feedstock is supplied from a short distance from the AD plant (5-10 as in the studied plant, transport (including also transport of digestate) does not contribute significantly to the overall environmental impacts.

The authors are grateful to the managers and operators of the anaerobic digestion plant and to Tommaso Gal- lingani for having provided the data necessary for the study.
